title: Randomness and the law of large numbers
intro: In the previous lesson we agreed that every event has a probability, and learned to combine them. But where does that probability come from, and where can you actually "see" it in the data? It turns out you can't spot it in a single flip — it emerges only over a long series: the more repetitions, the closer the observed share gets to the true probability. That is the law we'll explore on the simplest experiment — a coin flip.

beats.text: A coin lands heads or tails — you cannot call it in advance. But the event has a probability: a fair coin comes up heads half the time on average, p = 0.5 (the yellow dashed line). On the left, the running share of heads as flips accumulate; on the right, a frequency histogram of outcomes with target dashes at p and 1−p. Press "+10 flips".
After 10 flips the share of heads is almost never exactly 50%.
beats.predict: What happens to the share of heads if you keep flipping — hundreds, thousands of times?
beats.reveal: The share levels out and crawls toward 0.5. That is the law of large numbers: the more trials, the closer the observed share gets to the true probability. Press "+100 flips" a few times and watch the line.

beats.text: The key observation: over a few flips the share jumps around and can lie badly — even 80% heads in a row. Over large numbers it stabilizes. That is why a small sample deceives and a large one can be trusted.
Move the probability p, say to 0.30 (a biased coin). Now the share converges not to 0.5 but to 0.30. Probability IS the number the share tends to over the long run.
beats.predict: Five tails came up in a row. Do you think heads is now more likely, to "even out" the balance?
beats.reveal: No — the coin has no memory of past flips, and the next one is still 50/50. Expecting heads to "catch up" after a run of tails is the gambler's fallacy. The share evens out not because the imbalance is deliberately compensated, but because new flips simply dissolve it into the larger mass.

beats.text: Convergence is slow: to cut the typical wobble of the share in half you need four times more flips (the spread falls as 1/√n). So going from 100 observations to 400 stabilizes the estimate noticeably, while 100 → 110 barely does. We will meet this √n again when we measure how precise a sample estimate is.

definitions.text: the share of cases in which the event happens over a very long run. 0 — never, 1 — always.
one random observation — for example, one coin flip.
as trials accumulate, the observed share approaches the true probability.
to cut the typical error of a share in half, you need four times more observations.

practice: A direct consequence for analytics: the figure "we have 50% conversion" from five visitors means almost nothing — there are far too few trials, and the share swings wildly from case to case. On five thousand the same share is already close to the true one, and can be trusted.
So sample size is not a formality but a condition of reliability: it is what turns random noise into a stable estimate.
realLife: Casinos and insurers live off the law of large numbers: a single game or claim can lose money, but over a million events the total is predictable and stable.
Polls too: the more respondents, the closer the measured share is to what all people actually think.
nextLabel: Randomness deceives in subtler ways too: rare events and conditional probability → Conditional probability
